title: Global Economic Challenges Following the Iran War
content:
The failure to rebalance global economies is increasingly seen as a function of political short-sightedness. Former IMF chief economist Maurice Obstfeld notes that during prosperous periods, governments often fail to run surpluses and reduce their mounting debts. Since the US-Israeli attack against Iran began, some governments responded by spending more on fuel subsidies to contain the fallout. Others, however, simply cannot afford to do so, leaving everyday consumers to bear the burden of soaring prices. Ironically, the US, which initiated the conflict alongside Israel, may not be hit as hard as other vulnerable nations.

Even before the Iran war, IMF economists cautioned that reduced government resources and low growth weakened the global ability to respond to crises. The IMF still has plenty of firepower, with a massive lending capacity designed to protect countries in an increasingly volatile world. But critics worry that the fund has been sidelined in recent years and has lost its ability to foresee vulnerabilities. Central bankers face an acute challenge, particularly regarding the ongoing energy price shocks that destabilize local markets. They must now decide whether to raise interest rates to contain inflation or lower them to protect jobs.

Boosting interest rates presents its own set of dangers, as it increases the cost of servicing national debt. This further eats away the fiscal capacity required to respond effectively to future economic crises. In the US alone, total government debt held by the public is forecast to reach staggering new heights this year. Consequently, annual interest payments are on track to exceed a trillion dollars, severely limiting government flexibility. Ultimately, these compounding financial pressures threaten to permanently reshape the global economic landscape.
